policy_statement: Increase natural forest carbon sequestration capacity by reducing degradation and enhancing regeneration.
mechanism_description: Reducing natural forest degradation and enhancing regeneration increases forest carbon sequestration, helping the sector move toward a net sink.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem enhancement (reduced degradation + increased regeneration) → ↑ forest carbon stocks/sinks → ↓ net atmospheric CO₂ (↓ radiative forcing)
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in system: Forest management/land-use is altered to reduce degrading activities (e.g., unsustainable logging, encroachment, repeated disturbance) and to promote natural regeneration and recovery of native forests.  
(2) Mediating process: Healthier, less-disturbed forests accumulate biomass and soil organic carbon more rapidly and retain existing carbon by lowering disturbance-driven carbon losses (reduced decomposition/combustion and reduced harvest-related emissions).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net increase in carbon sequestration and storage, yielding net CO₂ removals and/or avoided CO₂ emissions from forest carbon loss.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increased net CO₂ removals via higher forest carbon sequestration (and avoided loss of existing forest carbon).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Ecosystem condition improves → forest carbon sink strengthens → atmospheric CO₂ decreases.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While these actions can also improve resilience (biodiversity, water regulation, reduced fire risk) and resource efficiency, the defining and largest climate effect across this mechanism is biological carbon sequestration/storage—core to Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy’s main goal were hazard reduction (e.g., fire breaks) or timber efficiency, it might lean Adaptation/Resource Efficiency, but those are not inherent to this mechanism as defined (degradation reduction + regeneration to increase sequestration).  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that reduces natural forest degradation and enhances regeneration to increase sequestration operates through ecosystem carbon stock/sink enhancement, fitting Nature-Based Solutions consistently.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Co-benefits (biodiversity, livelihoods, adaptation) and exact intervention type (natural regeneration vs assisted restoration) require policy-level evidence and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could overlap with Mitigation if framed purely as “carbon removals” accounting, but the mechanism is explicitly ecosystem-based sequestration, so Nature-Based Solutions is the more consistent primary category; adaptation co-benefits (fire/flood/heat buffering) are common but secondary.
key_indicators: “turn it into net sink”; “increasing the capacity of natural forest in sequestering carbon”; “reducing degradation”; “enhancing the regeneration”; “by 2050”; “by 2030”
co_benefits: Biodiversity conservation; watershed protection/water regulation; soil conservation; livelihood and ecosystem services support
instance_edge_case_notes: Could alternatively be coded as a target-setting instrument due to the “net sink by 2050/2030” language, but the specific policy statement here is framed as an action/strategy (“depends primarily on the success of… actions”) rather than a quantified emissions/sequestration target or a defined implementing instrument.
